items.q: What makes the antigen-binding site of an antibody specific to one antigen?
items.correct: The variable region — its amino acid sequence (and thus shape) differs between antibodies, complementing a specific antigen.
items.misconception: All antibodies have the same binding site; specificity comes from where they go in the body.
items.random_wrong: Antibodies bind to any antigen they meet first.
items.why: Students miss the role of the variable region in determining specificity, thinking antibodies are interchangeable generic 'germ-killers'.

items.correct: A series of chemical reactions in cells that break down glucose to release energy as ATP.
items.misconception: Respiration means breathing — inhaling oxygen and exhaling carbon dioxide.
items.random_wrong: Respiration is the process by which plants make their own food using sunlight.
items.why: Everyday English equates 'respiration' with breathing; students rarely separate gas exchange from cellular metabolism.

items.q: Where do plants get most of their biomass from?
items.correct: From carbon dioxide in the air — the carbon in plant tissue comes mostly from CO2 fixed during photosynthesis.
items.misconception: Plants get most of their mass from the soil through their roots.
items.random_wrong: Plants gain most of their mass directly from sunlight, which has weight.
items.why: Roots in soil look like the source of food/mass, but actually most plant dry mass is carbon from atmospheric CO2.

items.q: The two-part Latin name of an organism is its:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: binomial name. “family name” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
It's Kingdom. “Species” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: How many classification levels in Kingdom → Species?
items.why: Exact answer: 7 levels. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
